title: Windows Domination
module_title: Windows Privilege Escalation Techniques and Exploitation
learning_objectives: Execute automated enumeration on Windows systems
Abuse registry and group policy configurations
Compare Windows vs Linux escalation approaches
scenario: Three Windows machines with different escalation vectors. Standard user access. Escalate to SYSTEM/Admin on all three.

scenario_context.setting: Three Windows systems with common misconfigurations
scenario_context.challenge: Achieve SYSTEM-level access on multiple machines
scenario_context.relevance: Windows environments use similar escalation patterns

subtasks.title: Automated Enumeration
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Run WinPEAS and PowerUp scripts on each machine. Analyse output. Identify top privilege escalation vectors. Annotate key findings.

subtasks.title: Machine 1: Service Exploitation
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Identify vulnerable service (unquoted path or weak permissions). Create malicious binary. Replace legitimate binary or place in path. Trigger service.

subtasks.title: Machine 2: Registry/Policy Exploit
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Check registry for AlwaysInstallElevated or other vulnerable settings. Create malicious MSI or exploit registry setting. Achieve SYSTEM access.
subtasks.evidence_required: SYSTEM shell evidence + registry configuration documented

subtasks.title: Machine 3: Token Impersonation
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Check for SeImpersonate token. Use Potato attack (RoguePotato, JuicyPotato, etc.) to impersonate SYSTEM. Achieve SYSTEM access.
subtasks.evidence_required: SYSTEM shell evidence + Potato attack methodology

subtasks.title: Cross-Platform Comparison
subtasks.detailed_guidance: Compare escalation vectors across Windows and Linux. Document different approaches, tool usage, and relative difficulty.

subtasks.title: Hardening Recommendations
subtasks.detailed_guidance: For each vulnerability type, provide Windows-specific remediation and hardening guidance.
subtasks.evidence_required: Windows hardening guide with specific recommendations

resources.name: Token Impersonation and Potato Attacks Reference

reflection_prompt: How do Windows escalation vectors differ from Linux? What patterns do you see across the three machines?

quiz.question: Why do unquoted service paths lead to privilege escalation?
options: Windows searches path for executables without quotes, allowing DLL hijacking
quiz.explanation: If a service path isn't quoted and contains spaces, Windows searches the path for a matching executable, allowing attackers to place malicious binaries in earlier paths.

quiz.question: What privilege does SeImpersonate token enable?
options: Allows impersonation of any user the system has tokens for
quiz.explanation: SeImpersonate allows a process to impersonate security tokens, enabling privilege escalation attacks like Potato attacks to SYSTEM level.

portfolio_artifacts: WinPEAS and PowerUp outputs (annotated, per machine)
Machine 1: Service exploitation proof and methodology
Machine 3: Token impersonation and Potato attack proof
mentor_notes: All 3 machines must be escalated to SYSTEM/Admin. WinPEAS/PowerUp analysis should be thorough. Comparison should show platform understanding. 48-hour deadline is tight for three escalations.
